title: Battery Storage Market Growth in 2023
summary_md: The grid-scale battery storage market set records in 2023, driven by falling costs and supportive policy.

sources.title: Batteries and Secure Energy Transitions (IEA, 2024)
sources.snippet: Global battery storage capacity additions reached 130 GW in 2023, a record annual addition. The cost of utility-scale lithium-ion batteries fell below $150 per kWh in 2023, down 90% from 2013 levels.

sources.title: Global Energy Storage Outlook (Wood Mackenzie, 2024)
sources.snippet: Renewable energy policy mandates were the primary driver of new battery storage procurement in 2023. China accounted for over 60% of global deployments.

sections.heading: Deployment Records
sections.body_md: The battery storage sector broke records in 2023. Global capacity additions reached 130 GW in 2023, the highest ever recorded.[^s1] Cost reductions continued apace, with utility-scale lithium-ion battery prices falling below $150 per kWh in 2023, a 90% decline from 2013 levels.[^s1]

sections.heading: Market Drivers
sections.body_md: Policy and economics both accelerated deployment. Renewable energy policy mandates were the primary driver of new battery storage procurement in 2023.[^s2] On a regional basis, the United States led global battery storage deployments in 2023, accounting for over 60% of new capacity.[^s2]

topic: LLM scaling laws and compute-optimal training, 2022
title: Chinchilla and the Compute-Optimal Frontier
summary_md: The Chinchilla paper overturned prior assumptions about large language model training, showing that data quantity matters as much as model size.

sources.title: Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Hoffmann et al., 2022)
sources.snippet: For compute-optimal training, the number of training tokens should scale in proportion to model parameters. A 70B parameter model should be trained on roughly 1.4 trillion tokens. The Chinchilla 70B model outperforms Gopher 280B using the same training compute budget.

sources.title: Scaling Laws for Neural Language Models (Kaplan et al., 2020)
sources.snippet: Model performance scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute. Larger models are more sample-efficient than smaller models trained for longer on the same compute budget.

sections.heading: Chinchilla Scaling Laws
sections.body_md: Hoffmann et al. (2022) established revised scaling principles. They found that compute-optimal training requires scaling training tokens proportionally to model parameters.[^s1] Concretely, a 70B parameter model requires roughly 1.4 trillion training tokens for compute-optimal performance.[^s1] The result was striking: Chinchilla 70B outperforms Gopher 280B despite using the same training compute.[^s1]

sections.heading: Prior Scaling Research
sections.body_md: Kaplan et al. (2020) laid the groundwork with earlier scaling laws. They showed that model performance scales as a power law with model size, dataset size, and compute.[^s2] Their key finding was that smaller models trained on more data are always more sample-efficient than larger models trained on less data for the same compute budget.[^s2]
